beats.text: Here's matrix A — entries two, one, eight, seven.

Run elimination. The pivot sits at row one, column one — that's two. To kill the entry below it, compute the multiplier: eight divided by two equals four. Row two becomes row two minus four times row one. Eight, seven becomes zero, three.
That's U — upper triangular: two, one, zero, three. But elimination also built something else.
Every multiplier used gets stored in L — lower triangular. The diagonal holds ones. The single multiplier, four, drops into the entry at row two, column one. L reads: one, zero, four, one.
Check: L times U reconstructs A exactly. One, zero, four, one times two, one, zero, three — multiply it out — lands back on two, one, eight, seven.
New right-hand side? Reuse L and U — no elimination needed again. One factorization, unlimited solves. That's the payoff.
